author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Difficulty of Believing Original Sin. Man’s Vice is a Beast’s Nature.
title: Difficulty of Believing Original Sin. Man’s Vice is a Beast’s Nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: No one should feel surprise, and ask: “Why does God’s goodness create anything for the devil’s malignity to take possession of?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: The truth is, God’s gift is bestowed on the seminal elements of His creature with the same bounty wherewith “He maketh His sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: It is with so large a bounty that God has blessed the very seeds, and by blessing has constituted them.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Nor has this blessing been eliminated out of our excellent nature by a fault which puts us under condemnation.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Owing, indeed, to God’s justice, who punishes, this fatal flaw has so far prevailed, that men are born with the fault of original sin; but yet its influence has not extended so far as to stop the birth of men.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Just so does it happen in persons of adult age: whatever sins they commit, do not eliminate his manhood from man; nay, God’s work continues still good, however evil be the deeds of the impious.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For although “man being placed in honour abideth not; and being without understanding, is compared with the beasts, and is like them,” yet the resemblance is not so absolute that he becomes a beast.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: There is a comparison, no doubt, between the two; but it is not by reason of nature, but through vice—not vice in the beast, but in nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For so excellent is a man in comparison with a beast, that man’s vice is beast’s nature; still man’s nature is never on this account changed into beast’s nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: God, therefore, condemns man because of the fault wherewithal his nature is disgraced, and not because of his nature, which is not destroyed in consequence of its fault.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Heaven forbid that we should think beasts are obnoxious to the sentence of condemnation!

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: It is only proper that they should be free from our misery, inasmuch as they cannot partake of our blessedness.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: What, then, is there surprising or unjust in man’s being subjected to an impure spirit—not on account of nature, but on account of that impurity of his which he has contracted in the stain of his birth, and which proceeds, not from the divine work, but from the will of man;—since also the impure spirit itself is a good thing considered as spirit, but evil in that it is impure?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For the one is of God, and is His work, while the other emanates from man’s own will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: The stronger nature, therefore, that is, the angelic one, keeps the lower, or human, nature in subjection, by reason of the association of vice with the latter.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Accordingly the Mediator, who was stronger than the angels, became weak for man’s sake.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: So that the pride of the Destroyer is destroyed by the humility of the Redeemer; and he who makes his boast over the sons of men of his angelic strength, is vanquished by the Son of God in the human weakness which He assumed.
